sentences.text: I think, after the liberation, it took about three months.
Yes.
And, first-- first of all, when the Americans came, I never seen American before.
And they came on a tremendous tanks with their-- in their uniforms, helmets, and their earphones.
And I-- I've heard people around me say, they are Americans.
But to me, they looked like giants from a different planet.
And I remember they start throwing chewing gum, and chocolates, and cigarettes-- whatever they could.
And people were throwing themselves on all the food.
And there were a lot of people.
They were sick.
And they looked like skeletons.
They used to call them Muselmanns.
And I-- I-- I was sort of-- I never rush to anything.
I was always waiting for somebody to push me into things.
I was never volunteering.
I was, I guess, like a frightened child.
Well, somebody gave me a piece of chocolate.
But what happened-- the people were dying as they were eating.
The people were weighing-- you know, like, an adult, 40 pounds, 45 pounds.
They were dying of dysentery.
So right away the American Red Cross stop the foods.
When they came into the camp, they set up the provision for delousing and-- and disinfections.
And they were burning everything that we wore.
And they issue-- they had, like, fatigues-- army stuff.
And, for the women, I received a white uniform-- nurse's uniform.
And I was helping in a hospital with few other women that were still healthy and capable, to help the others to get well.
I remember a lot of people were taken and shipped to Switzerland to recuperate.
And that was the time, I guess-- I was told, at that time-- because I wasn't aware.
There were no radios available to me.
But that was the time where United States was in war with Japan or went into war.

Because they offered me-- if I would be a nurse and stay with the United States group-- and I don't know how serious they were about it, because I certainly was a minor-- then I would become a citizen of the United States, right away.
Well, they showed us.
They train us right there.
It wasn't very extensive training, but I remember they trained me how to cover a wound, how to roll up a Band-Aid, how to apply a bandage, how to tend to the sick people.
And that was the extent that I remember now.
Because I certainly did not stay there very long.
I remember I never seen a color people, unless I saw Shirley Temple movie, and I saw the mammy in the movie.
But in person I never did.
And being in this doctors and nurses atmosphere, there was one doctor-- a Jewish doctor that command English and spoke Polish.
No.
I don't even remember his face.
I remember the incident.
And he took a few of us with him to go to the American area in a tent where they had supplies for the hospital.
And as we walked in, there were some color doctors-- Negroes.
And they were talking in English, which I couldn't understand.
And one-- and they smiled.
And one of them put his arm-- I mean, his hand-- on my shoulder.
And I automatically went like this.
I lower my shoulder.
So the doctor that came with us told me, in Polish, don't do that.
You will insult them.
He won't leave you a spot.
This is the color of his skin.
Incidents like this you never forget-- how naive, how-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --I didn't know how uneducated and uniformed we were.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then I remember they ask us questions-- where we would like to go-- if we would like to go home, back to Poland.
And naturally, my uncles were liberated the same time, so we could communicate more freely.
One of my uncle stayed in Germany.
And the other uncle went and took care of me.
We left Mauthausen together.
And that's the Uncle Marion.
We went together to Poland, to Krakow.
Well, I went with my uncle, first to Podgorze, because he lived there before the war, before even ghetto.
He had a candy factory.
And he gave the factory away to some workers of his so they could hide his daughter, my cousin.
And two weeks before the war ended, she was shot by a Polish-- a Polish policeman.
They found out, and they took her to the forest, and they shot her.
So he wanted to go back to see the Polish people that had her in hiding for all this time.
And these people were also in danger with their own lives.
But then in Krakow was nothing.
We gather in Katowice, which is another city in Poland.
It's actually Oberschlesien.
It's closer to the German borders.
And this is where all the family is supposed to meet, if we ever come back.
So this is where I met-- my uncle took me, and this is where my parents, that were liberated before-- this is where I met them and a couple--
My mother was liberated in Czestochowa, which is not a concentration camp but a labor camp.
It was in Poland.
The same-- the same.
My mother and father were together.
But we were separated, the children.
They never knew where I was, or my brother.
And my brother--
No, not until after the liberation and they saw me coming.
I remember--
Yeah.
I remember when my mother saw me-- her excitement, her crying.
She didn't know where to put me and what to do with me.
We didn't know that my brother was alive.
And he survived Dachau.
I didn't know he was alive.
Dachau and Mauthausen are not that far away from each other.
We had no idea.
And after I was home for a couple weeks-- we have a family whistle that was taught to us when we were children, by my father.
His family had it, and now it's in our family.
And my sons have it.
It's a whistle.
Whenever we are in Disneyland and we want to find each other, or in shopping center, they respond to this whistle.
The children know it.
And it's, I guess-- my brother whistle as he was walking.
And we were on the second floor.
I thought my mother would jump out of the window when she saw him coming.
He was in good spirits.
He was a young man.
And, oddly enough, I never been to Dachau, but my son just recently visited Germany and Dachau.
And he says, mother, I've seen some pictures-- and at Dachau, I guess they have, like, a museum there-- that I swear it was Uncle Freddy in it.
He didn't take a picture of it.
But if God will let me, and I ever go back, I'd like to go to Dachau to just see if my brother-- my brother's picture is there.
Although he survived, and I have some pictures of him.
But unfortunately, he died as a very young man.
He died in 1969 of cancer.
Shall I whistle?
[BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just two long sounds-- and this is how we find each other in the crowds.
And we know it's our whistle.
And that was the whistle.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
The Russians were in Poland.
And my parents did not want us-- for us, the lifestyle.
We found we were freed, and-- and we wanted freedom.
So I remember-- and there were other members of family that gathered also in Katowice.
One of my aunts were smuggled from ghetto, and she was with the Polish underground.
That's why we knew how-- and we were told as children, if whoever comes back, to come to Katowice, hopefully, we find each other there.
And yes, she survived, and she was the base for all of us when we were coming back.
She is alive now, in her early '80s, in Tel Aviv.
And her name?
Her name is Sabinka.
Her maiden name was Immergluck.
And-- do you know?
I have a--
--mental block.
[LAUGHS]
It's not [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is my mother's family.
Goldkorn-- Sabina Goldkorn.
Yeah.
She has a daughter and grandchildren.
I came in 1949.
No, no.
No.
I came with my brother on a student quota.
You had to wait to enter the United States.
I really, truly-- when I was in Germany, in Munich, there were other people from Israel, at that time, Palestine.
That's before '48.
I wanted to go to Israel with the Aliyah Bet.
And, again, my parents said, let's keep together.
I don't want us to be separated again.
So you listen to your parents.
We stayed together.
And you had to apply as a displaced person.
Some people went to Canada.
We came to the United States.
And we had no family in this country.
I came with my brother first.
We came without our parents.
But our parents would follow.
And naturally, the organization-- we came through the Joint.
When I-- I arrived to Boston, but my sort of contract-- I guess that's what it was, just that-- for the quota-- we were assigned to go to Omaha, Nebraska.
So, after arriving to Boston, we traveled to Omaha, Nebraska.
And it was winter.
And it didn't smell very well to me.
It's-- I didn't know what Omaha, Nebraska, was.
But later on I understood that it's a cow industry.
We stayed there about a week.
I arrived with just a little, very light jacket.
And the-- the ladies that took care of us-- first, they made a mistake.
They thought that my brother and I, we are a couple.
So they book us in a one-room hotel.
And as soon as they found out, they book us in a two small rooms.
[LAUGHS] It-- it was funny, at that time.
But we had a very good friend that also survived, and we knew him from ghetto.
His name was Jack [NON-ENGLISH].
He lives in New York-- a very good friend of ours and my brothers, and he sort of was like our brother.
He had a family in Detroit, Michigan.
And when he heard that we arrived to this country, he said, why don't you come to Detroit, Michigan?
And my cousins will guarantee that you won't be a burden on the government, and so on and so forth.
So the transition took place from Omaha, Nebraska, to Detroit, Michigan.
And this is where we lived, in Detroit, Michigan.
And my parents came about seven month afterwards with my little sister that was born in Germany after the war.
At that time, she must have been maybe 3, 3 and a 1/2 years old.
And we lived in a one-bedroom apartment.
It was very difficult to get apartments.
It was after the war in this country too.
I worked and went to night school.
I did not go back to school to finish my profession that I started in Germany through ORT.
It was to become a dental technician.
I also took sewing, because my mother never knew how to sew.
She said, you have to know how to thread a needle.
So I also learn how to sew.
And I-- I was assigned to a class that designs men's underwear.
It's funny, but that was the first, I think, designing, at that time, in Germany that start the boxer shorts with a flyer.
[LAUGHS]
So I remember I learned this also through the ORT.
And we had German teachers and professors.
So, when I came to this country, I didn't go full-time to school.
I felt I didn't know English enough, so I had to go to night school to learn English.
Which I graduated-- Central High, in Detroit.
And my brother--
Well, I went to night school with a lot of other refugees.
So I was a displaced person.
I was a refugee.
I used to-- I was a [NON-ENGLISH].
There were many names.
I just know that most of the people adopted extended families-- worked very hard and educated themselves.
It didn't come easy.
And they were never a burden on their government.
The Jewish agencies were very supportive to many families.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know that my family, other than was brought by the Jewish agency, never, never took anything again.
We worked, and we support ourselves.
In Michigan, I worked in one of the better dress-designing places, as a finisher, until I made a mistake.
And I didn't work there anymore.
I also worked as a-- a salesperson in the J.L.
Hudson department in Detroit, which was one of the largest department store there.
I got married in 1952.
Well, it was a blind date.
No.
My husband came to United States in 1945 from Cuba.
He's a Cuban Jew that came with his family to immigrate to the United States.
They had a lot of family here in this country.
And we met through some mutual friends.
He was in the United States Army in World War II, but he was discharged after nine months, after the war was over.
And seven weeks after our honeymoon, he was drafted again, and he went for two years to Korea.
So he was in the Korean War right after we were married.
No.
I had a lot of new friends.
Most of them were also survivors that I knew.
We used to go to high school together.
We used to have our groups together.
And we felt comfortable being together.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I felt the most comfortable among the survivors, even though I don't know them.
But when we get together, it's like you find a lost brother or a sister.
Our stories and our history that we went through-- it's so much the same.
We feel differently, but, unfortunately, the stories are the same.
Oh, definitely.
Oh, definitely.
Yes, he wanted to hear it.
But when you're young, you hear the stories, but I don't think that they are as penetrating as when you have the full awareness and maturity about life.
I didn't tell my children my story until they were a little bit older.
I didn't want to frighten them.
I didn't know how to start telling them my story.
I wanted them to be strong, and confident, and have trust in human beings-- in people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The disappointment, for me, was very great, too, and still is, in human beings.
I continue to have faith.
But I'm still disappointed.
When-- I thought I will be freed when I was free.
I thought I find freedom for all people-- that I wouldn't see any suffering and starvation.
But it's not so.
There is still a lot of suffering, a lot of prejudice-- a lot of hurt-- hunger in this world.
And the purpose of me sitting here today-- which, by the way, is-- is certainly not an enjoyment for me-- so people can know what happened.
It didn't leave me scarred.
I am scarred.
I, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --I don't want to talk about it, because I believe in better world to come.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But it leaved us all scarred.
[CRYING] You know, I'm not emotional for me.
I am emotional for those-- [CRYING] --I feel emotional and sorry for those that didn't make it-- and for many of those that are scarred.
My own mother-- she never recuperated.
She functions.
And she goes from day to day.
But to lose her family, to be uprooted, it's a horrible thing.
I am very grateful.
I appreciate what I have.
I appreciate this country.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]
